coredata.dc:title: One-stone-four-birds strategy to construct magnetic mesoporous nano-ratio fluorescence sensor for efficient discovery of PSD95-nNOS uncouplers

coredata.dc:description: It is effective through uncoupling postsynaptic density protein-95 (PSD95) and neuronal nitric oxide synthase (nNOS) for treating ischemic stroke with high disability, mortality and recurrence rate. However, there are no reliable uncouplers for PSD95-nNOS in clinical practice, and the established screening models for discovering PSD95-nNOS uncouplers have high costs and interference. In this study, epoxy functional fluorescent magnetic mesoporous silica (EFMMS) was synthesized by combining carbon dots (CDs), magnetic mesoporous silica and epoxy functionalization. Ni-EFMMS was further constructed by loading nickel ions to simultaneously purify and covalently immobilize His-tagged neuronal nitric oxide synthase (His-nNOS) and then couple with green fluorescent proteins (GFP) labeled PSD95, finally fabricating nano-biosensor (PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS). The nanomaterial was well constructed and characterized, and it distinctly constituted stable ratio fluorescence between CDs and GFP-PSD95. The ratio fluorescence intensity of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS remained stable even after separately mixing with metal ions, amino acids and natural impurities, and the loss of ratio fluorescence intensity was less than 20% even at pH 4 or 10. PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS still kept the ratio fluorescence intensity above 90% after being recycled for seven times. Three compounds (gnetol, baicalein and emodin-8-O-β-D-glucoside) with strong uncoupling efficiency were screened from fourteen natural active ingredients. Furthermore, the theoretical feasibility of uncoupling was verified by molecular docking. In general, this study provides a novel “one-stone-four-birds strategy” to fabricate magnetic mesoporous nano-ratio fluorescence sensor for efficiently and accurately discovering PSD95-nNOS uncoupling active substances.

head.ce:title: One-stone-four-birds strategy to construct magnetic mesoporous nano-ratio fluorescence sensor for efficient discovery of PSD95-nNOS uncouplers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is effective through uncoupling postsynaptic density protein-95 (PSD95) and neuronal nitric oxide synthase (nNOS) for treating ischemic stroke with high disability, mortality and recurrence rate. However, there are no reliable uncouplers for PSD95-nNOS in clinical practice, and the established screening models for discovering PSD95-nNOS uncouplers have high costs and interference. In this study, epoxy functional fluorescent magnetic mesoporous silica (EFMMS) was synthesized by combining carbon dots (CDs), magnetic mesoporous silica and epoxy functionalization. Ni-EFMMS was further constructed by loading nickel ions to simultaneously purify and covalently immobilize His-tagged neuronal nitric oxide synthase (His-nNOS) and then couple with green fluorescent proteins (GFP) labeled PSD95, finally fabricating nano-biosensor (PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS). The nanomaterial was well constructed and characterized, and it distinctly constituted stable ratio fluorescence between CDs and GFP-PSD95. The ratio fluorescence intensity of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS remained stable even after separately mixing with metal ions, amino acids and natural impurities, and the loss of ratio fluorescence intensity was less than 20% even at pH 4 or 10. PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS still kept the ratio fluorescence intensity above 90% after being recycled for seven times. Three compounds (gnetol, baicalein and emodin-8-O-β-D-glucoside) with strong uncoupling efficiency were screened from fourteen natural active ingredients. Furthermore, the theoretical feasibility of uncoupling was verified by molecular docking. In general, this study provides a novel “one-stone-four-birds strategy” to fabricate magnetic mesoporous nano-ratio fluorescence sensor for efficiently and accurately discovering PSD95-nNOS uncoupling active substances.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ischemic stroke often occurs when the blood supply vessels to the brain are blocked, accounting for about 87 % of all stroke cases [1,2]. Ischemia triggers ischemic cascade reaction that causes adverse events such as excitotoxicity [3], oxidative stress [4], cell death (apoptosis or necrosis), inflammation, and immune responses [5–7], ultimately leading to irreversible brain damage.

Cerebral ischemia leads to over-activation of N-methyl-D-aspartate receptors (NMDARs), which induces neuronal nitric oxide synthase (nNOS) combined with postsynaptic density protein-95 (PSD95) to catalyze the production of a large amount of nitric oxide (NO) [8–10]. The studies have indicated that interference in PSD95-nNOS interaction can selectively block the connection between NMDAR and NO production without affecting synaptic activity or calcium ion inflow, avoiding the adverse reactions of directly blocking NMDAR and inhibiting nNOS [8,11,12].

4-(3,5-Dichloro-2-hydroxybenzylamino)-2-hydroxybenzoic acid (ZL006) has been designed to selectively block PSD95-nNOS binding, but the disadvantages such as fast metabolism and low permeability across blood brain barrier still hinder its further clinical application [13,14]. Additionally, some natural active ingredients could inhibit apoptosis, oxidative stress, and inflammatory response, and promote angiogenesis and neurogenesis, which possess great potential to treat ischemic stroke as PSD95-nNOS uncouplers [1,15,16]. Due to the structural complexity of natural active ingredients, there is an urgent need to construct a sensitive sensing system to efficiently discover PSD95-nNOS uncouplers.

In the effort to discover PSD95-nNOS uncouplers, we had synthesized magnetic metal–organic frameworks to immobilize His-tagged nNOS and green fluorescent protein labeled PSD95 (GFP-PSD95) as a screening platform for potential PSD95-nNOS uncouplers [17]. However, the fragile immobilization of His-tag could make His-nNOS be apt to fall off from the immobilized carrier. Moreover, the single-fluorescence detection only based on GFP could influence the uniformity of samples and be prone to external interference, which affected the reliability of the experimental results. Therefore, it is necessary to design a new screening strategy to firmly immobilize His-nNOS and reliably detect the uncoupling of His-nNOS and GFP-PSD95.

It is well known that His tags could interact with metal ions (Ni2+, Cu2+, Co2+) through non-covalent binding to separate and purify recombinant proteins [18,19], and epoxide carriers have been widely used for covalent immobilization of various proteins [20–22]. Based on the application of His tags and epoxy groups in protein immobilization, the approach to fabricating the multifunctional epoxy carrier is proposed, in which part of epoxy groups undergo ring-opening to bond with Ni2+ to physically adsorb His-nNOS, and generate proximity effect that selectively concentrates His-nNOS around the immobilized material. Meanwhile, the unreacted epoxy rings establish covalent connections with the protein, ensuring the firm immobilization of His-nNOS and achieving efficient purification and stabilization of nNOS. At present, many materials have been used as carriers for protein immobilization, such as magnetic nanoparticles [23,24], mesoporous silica [25], metal–organic framework (MOF) [26–28], hydrogen-bonded organic framework (HOF) [29] and covalent organic framework (COF) [30,31]. The combination of magnetic nanoparticles and mesoporous silica to prepare magnetic mesoporous silica (MMS) will possess the advantages like magnetism, easily modified surface, good biocompatibility and stability. Furthermore, the synthesis of epoxy functionalized MMS as carriers to simultaneously purify and immobilize His-nNOS has not been reported and thereby designed in this study.

Compared with traditional protein detection methods such as western blot and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, fluorescence detection is simple, convenient, low cost, rapid reaction, high sensitivity and good selectivity [32–34]. Among fluorescent proteins, GFP is widely used for protein fluorescence detection like GFP-PSD95 [35]. However, the single fluorescence signal change is easily affected by the operation and external environment, therefore, ratio fluorescence is established to evaluate the change of protein concentration.

In this study, epoxy functional fluorescent magnetic mesoporous silica (EFMMS) was prepared through embedding red carbon dots (rCDs) into MMS and performing epoxidation on MMS, and then further loaded nickel ions to construct Ni-EFMMS, which was used for simultaneous purification and covalent immobilization of His-nNOS and coupling with GFP-PSD95 to fabricate nano-biosensor (PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS). The nanomaterial could be used to screen PSD95-nNOS uncouplers via detecting the ratio fluorescence intensity between rCDs and PSD95 to evaluate the uncoupling efficiency. PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was systematically characterized and evaluated, and the results demonstrated that PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS could successfully screen out uncouplers with excellent uncoupling efficiency, which provides a reliable “one-stone-four-birds” strategy for discovering PSD95-nNOS uncoupling agents in vitro (Scheme 1 ).

Urea, ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O), polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG 200) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA), iron (Ⅲ) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), epichlorohydrin (ECH), iron (Ⅱ) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O) were purchased from Macklin. N-(3-(dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), trimethylamine (Et3N), 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS), iminodiacetic acid (IDA), nickel sulfate hexahydrate (NiSO4·6H2O), imidazole, glycine, and 7-amino-4-methyl-3-coumarinylacid (AMCA) were purchased from Aladdin. Formic acid (FA), ethyl alcohol (EtOH), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetone, toluene, methanol (MeOH)) and other organic reagents are analytically pure. Yeast powder, peptone and other medium raw materials were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The recombinant plasmids pcDNA3.1-His-nNOS and pcDNA3.1-GFP-PSD95 were constructed by Nanjing Geneppl Technology Co., Ltd. The UNlQ-200 column plasmid DNA medium volume extraction kit was purchased from Shanghai Shengong Bioengineering Co., Ltd. Lipo 293TM transfection reagent was bought from Biyuntian Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Bradford protein quantitative kit was purchased from Jiangsu Kangwei Century Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All the standard products were purchased from Nanjing Yuanbaofeng Pharmaceutical Technology. ZL006 was kindly offered by the department of medicinal chemistry, Nanjing Medical University (Nanjing, China).

LB medium was obtained by dissolving 4.0 g sodium chloride, 2.0 g yeast powder and 4.0 g peptone in 400 mL water and sterilized at 121 °C for 30 min. 12 μL ampicillin aqueous solution (5 %, m/v) was added into 10 mL LB medium, and then 10 μL recombinant Escherichia Coli was added and cultured at 37 °C, 200 rpm for 12 h, subsequently, Escherichia Coli was collected to extract recombinant plasmid with UNlQ-200 column plasmid DNA medium volume extraction kit.

The nNOS (gene ID: 24598) derives from Rattus norvegicus and postsynaptic density protein-95 is from Homo sapiens (PSD95, gene ID: 1742). Protein-induced expression was performed in human embryonal kidney (HEK) 293 T cells. Recombinant plasmids including pcDNA3.1-His-nNOS and pcDNA3.1-GFP-PSD95 were separately transfected into HEK 293 T with transfection reagent Lipo 293TM, and cultured in the medium at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 for 2 days, then the medium was discarded. The crude protein solution was obtained after ice cracking with deionized water and centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 15 min. The extracted His-nNOS and GFP-PSD95 proteins were verified by western blot analysis.

According to previous protocol [36,37], rCDs were synthesized by adding 3.0 g citric acid and 6.0 g urea in 30 mL formic acid and being treated at 160 °C for 4 h. The dark reddish-brown suspension (10 mL) was mixed with 20 mL ethanol and then centrifuged to obtain rCDs at 10,000 r min−1 for 5 min, washed by ethanol for two times again. After that, rCDs were dispersed in 2 mL DMSO, the appropriate amount of EDC and NHS were added and activated for 30 min, then 5 μL APTES was added and the suspension was shaken at room temperature for 12 h without centrifugation or other treatment. APTES-CDS was obtained by centrifugation and stored at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of epoxy-functionalized fluorescent magnetic mesoporous silica (EFMMS)
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized as follows: 0.1 g Fe3O4 was dispersed in the mixed solution of 80 mL ethanol and 20 mL deionized water, adding 1 mL NH3·H2O and subsequently 100 μL TEOS, the solution reacted at room temperature for 6 h, Fe3O4@nSiO2 was magnetically separated and washed with PBS [38]. The precipitate was dispersed in the solution containing 80 mL deionized water, 60 mL ethanol, 1 mL NH3·H2O and 0.3 g CTAB, and stirred at room temperature for 30 min, and then the above APTES-CDs and 400 μL TEOS were slowly added. After the reaction continually stirred at room temperature for 4 h, 100 μL GPTMS was slowly added and reacted for 2 h [39]. The product was collected by magnetic separation and dispersed in acetone to be refluxed at 80 °C for 2 days, the operation was repeated twice to collect EFMMS and then dry it in vacuum at 45 °C.

0.1 g EFMMS was reacted with 10 mL IDA (0.5 mM, pH 8) at room temperature for 1 h and separated by magnet. After being washed with deionizing water, IDA-EFMMS was incubated with NiSO4·6H2O (0.5 M, 10 mL) at room temperature for 2 h to obtain the loading nickel EFMMS, that is Ni-EFMMS [40]. Through magnetic separation and washing with deionized water, Ni-EFMMS were collected and dried in vacuum at 45 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous purification and immobilization of His-nNOS on Ni-EFMMS
ce:section.ce:para: 3.0 mg Ni-EFMMS was uniformly dispersed in 300 μL His-nNOS phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 1.25 mg/mL, pH 8) and incubated at room temperature for 24 h. The residual His-nNOS was collected and the magnetic material were then washed with PBS to obtain His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS. It was dispersed in 300 μL imidazole solution (0.5 M) and subsequently 300 μL glycine solution (1 M) was added, and each of them was incubated at 4 °C for 2 h. The non-covalently immobilized His-nNOS was removed by competitive adsorption of imidazole to Ni2+. On the other hand, the excess epoxides were blocked with high concentration of glycine to prevent interference to subsequent experiments and storage processes. The protein concentrations in the supernatants were determined by Bradford method.

The following formula (1) is used to calculate the covalent protein loading ratio of the material:
(1)

Among them, C0 represents initial His-nNOS concentration, C1 denotes His-nNOS concentration in the supernatant after immobilization on Ni-EFMMS for 24 h, and C2 represents His-nNOS concentration in the supernatant after imidazole elution.

According to our previous protocol [17], 3.0 mg His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was uniformly dispersed in 300 μL GFP-PSD95 PBS buffer (1.25 mg/mL, pH 8), and cultured at 4 °C, 175 rpm for 12 h. PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was collected by magnetic separation for uncoupling experiments within 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on fluorescence stability of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence stability of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was evaluated as follows: 3.0 mg PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was evenly dispersed in 300 μL PBS buffer and 300 μL PBS buffer containing 0.1 % DMSO, respectively. In addition, 300 μL His-nNOS solution was incubated with 300 μL GFP-PSD95 solution to get free PSD95-nNOS. The samples including free nNOS-PSD95, PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS dispersed in PBS buffer and PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS dispersed in PBS buffer with 0.1 % DMSO were allowed to stand at 4 °C for 0, 0.5, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24 h, respectively. Then the fluorescence intensity was measured for all samples. The fluorescence intensity of free GFP-PSD95-nNOS was determined at 506 nm, and the ratio fluorescence (RF) of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS in two different solvents is calculated according to formula (2). The experiment was repeated three times in parallel.
(2)
R a t i o
f l u o r e s c e n c e
( R F )
=
F 506
F 632
Among them, F506 and F632 are the fluorescence intensity of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS measured at 506 nm and 632 nm, respectively.

To inspect PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS pH stability, the nanomaterial (3.0 mg) was separately dispersed in PBS buffers at different pH like 4, 5, 6, 7.4, 8, 9 and 10. All of the suspensions were shaken at 4 °C and 125 rpm for 5 h, and magnetically collected. The RF of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS at different pH conditions was calculated according to formula (2), and the experiments were repeated for three times in parallel.

The disruptors such as metal ions, animo acids, celluose and gluocose were separately dissolved in PBS with the concentration 20 μM. 3.0 mg PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was dispersed in the above PBS solution (300 μL), respectively. After shaking at 4 °C and 125 rpm for 5 h, all of the supernatants were removed by magnetic separation, and the ratio fluorescence of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS in different interferant solutions was calculated according to formula (2). The experiments were repeated for three times in parallel.

The reusability was explored through using ZL006 as an uncoupler. 3.0 mg PSD95-nNOS /Ni-EFMMS was uniformly dispersed in ZL006 solution (4.0 mM, 300 μL) and shaken at 4°C and 125 rpm for 5 h to uncouple PSD95. The material was collected by magnetic separation and dispersed in 300 μL GFP-PSD95 protein solution (1.25 mg/mL, pH 8) to form PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS again. The ratio fluorescence of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was recorded. The procedure was repeated for seven times to compare the changes of ratio fluorescence intensity during the reuse process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS in screening uncouplers
ce:section.ce:para:
The known PSD95-nNOS uncouplers ZL006 was used as a positive control to further evaluate the feasibility of this strategy for screening PSD95-nNOS uncouplers. 3.0 mg PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was uniformly dispersed in 300 μL ZL006 solution with different concentrations of 0, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 and 4.0 mM, respectively. The ZL006 solution was shaken at 4 °C and 125 rpm for 5 h, and the supernatant was removed by magnetic separation. After washing with PBS, the RF values of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS were determined. The uncoupling efficiency is calculated according to formula (3):
(3)
U n c o u p l i n g
e f f i c i e n c y
( % )

× 100 %
Where RF0 is the ratio fluorescence after PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS interaction with 0 mM ZL006, and RFc is the ratio fluorescence after PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS interaction with c concentration ZL006. RFBlank is the ratio fluorescence of PSD95/Ni-EFMMS, in which Ni-EFMMS is only incubated with PSD95.

The uncoupling candidates were dissolved in PBS buffer containing 0.1 % DMSO at the concentration of 2.0 mM, subsequently each of candidate solution (300 μL) was incubated with PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS (3.0 mg) and shook at 4 °C and 125 rpm for 5 h. After magnetic separation and wash with PBS, the material was collected and its ratio fluorescence was determined, the uncoupling efficiency was calculated according to formula (3).

To minimize the interference of magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles to fluorescence, Fe3O4@nSiO2 was synthesized and the amount of TEOS was optimized. Fig. S1 showed the average particle size of Fe3O4 was about 50 nm, and the particle size of Fe3O4@nSiO2 increased when the dosage of TEOS was 50 μL. However, the particle size distributions appeared a little overlap between Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@nSiO2, which indicated that Fe3O4 might not have been completely encapsulated by SiO2. Therefore, the dosage of TEOS increased to 100 μL until the particle size distributions of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@nSiO2 had no intersection, which showed that Fe3O4 nanoparticles were fully coated by the nano-silica layer. Thus, the dosage of TEOS was selected to be 100 μL.

When Ni-EFMMS was prepared through IDA linking epoxy groups on EFMMS and Ni ions, Ni-EFMMS selectively coordinated with His-tag of His-nNOS and enriched the protein to the material via proximity effect, and the retained epoxy groups covalently bound with the protein for simultaneous purification and covalent immobilization of His-nNOS. Therefore, the concentration of IDA could affect open-ring ratio of epoxy groups on EFMMS surface as well as covalent loading efficiency, and the effect of IDA concentration on the covalent immobilization efficiency of His-nNOS was studied. As illustrated in Fig. S2a, when IDA concentration was 0 mM, the epoxy rings on EFMMS would open to covalently bind His-nNOS with covalent loading efficiency 48.31 %. With the gradual increase of IDA concentration, the covalent immobilization efficiency of His-nNOS showed a trend of increasing initially and then decreasing since IDA could chelate Ni2+ to selectively coordinate with His-nNOS and thereby enhance it to covalently bind with material at first, yet adding more IDA would result in less epoxy groups for covalent immobilization and accordingly decline covalent loading efficiency. Based on Fig. S2a, the covalent immobilization efficiency of His-nNOS was highest while IDA concentration was 0.5 mM, which was consequently regarded as the optimal open-ring concentration of the epoxy group.

Five metal ions including Fe3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+ and Zn2+ separately chelated to IDA to explore the effects of different metal ions on covalently immobilizing His-nNOS. The effects of different types of metal ions were investigated when IDA concentration, epoxide ring-opening time, and protein immobilization conditions were fixed (Fig. S2b). The affinity of different metal ions to His tags was different [19,41], which influenced the covalent immobilization efficiency of His-nNOS. It can be seen that Ni2+ had the highest affinity to His tags among the five metal ions, and Ni-EFMMS had the highest covalent immobilization efficiency for His-nNOS.

The morphology and structure of Ni-EFMMS and His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS were evaluated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1 a and b exhibited SEM images of Ni-EFMMS and His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS, respectively. Ni-EFMMS had uniform morphology with particle size about 250 nm. Compared with Ni-EFMMS, the surface of His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS became significantly rougher after protein immobilization, which suggested the immobilization of His-nNOS. Ni-EFMMS and His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS appeared mesoporous silica-coated layered structure with Fe3O4 as the core, indicating that His-nNOS didn’t damage the material structure after immobilization, which proved the stability of Ni-EFMMS (Fig. 1c and d).

His-nNOS was labeled with AMCA, and AMCA-His-nNOS and GFP-PSD95 were sequentially immobilized to synthesize PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS (Fig. 1e-h). The spatial distribution of proteins on the surface of the material was analyzed with confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). Among the components of labeled PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS, APTES-CDs should exhibit red fluorescence with AMCA-His-nNOS emitting blue fluorescence and GFP-PSD95 simultaneously emitting green fluorescence. Therefore, red, blue and green fluorescence were observed while His-nNOS and GFP-PSD95 were immobilized on Ni-EFMMS. As demonstrated in Fig. 1e-h, AMCA-His-nNOS and GFP-PSD95 had been immobilized on the surface of Ni-EFMMS due to the appearance of three colors fluorescence.

As demonstrated in Fig. 2 a and b, the results of FT-IR demonstrated that the synthesis of fluorescent magnetic mesoporous silica and the immobilization of proteins on its surface were smoothly performed. The characteristic absorption peak at 575 cm−1 was attributed to Fe-O vibration of Fe3O4 and appeared in all the other materials except APTES-CDs, which indicated that Fe3O4 as the initial substance and the magnetic core took part in all the subsequent synthetic materials and retained their magnetic properties. The stretching vibration peaks corresponding to Si-O-Si at 1080 cm−1 could be observed in all containing silica materials, which demonstrated the formation of nanosilica layer and mesoporous silica layer around Fe3O4 core. The absorption peak at 1634 cm−1 attributed to C=O in amide was observed in EFMMS and Ni-EFMMS, which conformed to the characteristic absorption peak of APTES-CDs and showed its existence in two mesoporous silica. The C-H characteristic absorption peaks at 2800–3000 cm−1 were also observed in EFMMS and Ni-EFMMS, indicating the epoxy functionalization of the material surface. Meanwhile, the peak at 973 cm−1 in EFMMS was related to C-O-C bond, however, this peak wasn’t observed in Fe3O4@nSiO2 due to the absence of epoxy group and the peak intensity was weaker in Ni-EFMMS compared with EFMMS owing to partial epoxy groups chelating Ni2+ by IDA.

As described in Fig. 2b, the immobilization of nNOS and GFP-PSD95 on the surface of the material didn’t damage the structure of the material due to the presence of characteristic peaks. The characteristic absorption broad peak near 1634 cm−1 in nNOS/Ni-EFMMS corresponded to C=O of APTES-CDs and His-nNOS, and both the broad peak intensity of nNOS/Ni-EFMMS and PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS enhanced after binding nNOS and PSD95, which fully indicated the immobilization of proteins.

The crystal structure of each step of synthetic material was characterized by XRD (Fig. 2c). The diffraction peaks in Fe3O4, Fe3O4@nSiO2 and EFMMS were in good agreement with the normal characteristic diffraction of Fe3O4 inverse spinel structure, which demonstrated that Fe3O4 was well-packaged and stitched in a multilayer structure. In addition to the characteristic peaks of Fe3O4, the wide peaks of EFMMS at 15°∼25° were attributed to amorphous silica shells and disordered graphite structures. There was a strong diffraction peak between 1° and 2° in the low-angle XRD pattern of EFMMS, representing the two-dimensional mesoporous structure of the outer silica (Fig. 2d). Fig. 2e showed the wide-angle XRD patterns of EFMMS, Ni-EFMMS and His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS, respectively. The diffraction peaks of three materials were in good consistency, indicating that the crystal structure of the material didn’t significantly change after protein immobilization.

In order to further prove the synthesis of fluorescent magnetic mesoporous silica and immobilization of His-nNOS, the specific surface area and pore structure of the material were analyzed by N2 adsorption–desorption test. As shown in Fig. 2f and Table 1 , compared with Fe3O4 (67.28 m2/g) and Fe3O4@nSiO2 (62.25 m2/g), EFMMS produced the larger specific surface area (230.73 m2/g) owing to its mesoporous structure. The BET surface area (106.00 m2/g) of Ni-EFMMS decreased after opening the epoxy ring to chelate Ni ions, while the surface area of His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS significantly decreased to 25.07 m2/g due to the immobilization of His-nNOS blocking the mesoporous cavity. The pore volumes of EFMMS, Ni-EFMMS and His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS sequentially descended as a result of material modification and protein immobilization (Table 1).

The magnetic strength of all the samples was evaluated (Fig. 2g). The saturation magnetization values of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@nSiO2, EFMMS, Ni-EFMMS, His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS, and PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS were 69.09, 52.88, 23.54, 22.51, 22.07 and 21.34 emu/g, respectively. The magnetic properties successively decreased, especially remarkably for EFMMS owing to the coating of nano silica and mesoporous silica layer. The weak magnetic changes of Ni-EFMMS, His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS and PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS indicated that epoxy modification and protein immobilization didn’t significantly decline the magnetic properties of materials. Consequently, PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS could be enriched and separated by magnets within 30 s, and quickly dispersed after slight shaking, which was conducive to the recycling and reuse of materials.

Fig. 2h showed the thermogravimetric curves of all samples in the range of 30 ∼ 800 °C. The ratios of weight loss for Fe3O4, Fe3O4@nSiO2, EFMMS, Ni-EFMMS, His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS and PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS were 2.28 %, 4.55 %, 25.69 %, 26.39 %, 35.29 % and 37.79 %, respectively. The slight weight loss of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@nSiO2 could result from the residual solvent. Nevertheless, the significant weight loss of EFMMS might be influenced by the assembly of the mesoporous silica layer, silanized CDs and the addition of epoxy groups. The weight loss ratio of Ni-EFMMS was slenderly higher than that of EFMMS due to IDA modification and metal ion chelation. Compared with Ni-EFMMS, His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS and PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS exhibited further weight loss, indicating the immobilization of His-nNOS and GFP-PSD95.

Additionally, the expression of His-nNOS and GFP-PSD95 was verified by western blot analysis, as well as the fixation of two proteins on Ni-EFMMS. As shown in Fig. S3, the bands at 170 kDa and 130 kDa were consistent with the molecular weight of His-nNOS and GFP-PSD95, indicating the successful expression of His-nNOS and GFP-PSD95. Compared with lanes 1 and 3, the target strips became thinner in grayscale for lanes 2 and 4, which demonstrated the purification of His-nNOS via Ni-EFMMS absorption and GFP-PSD95 successfully coupling with His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS.

Therefore, according to the above results, His-nNOS had been smoothly purified from the crude protein solution and immobilized while mixing with Ni-EFMMS. This process was unveiled via simulation (Fig. 2i). Since His-tag of nNOS produced an interaction with Ni ions of Ni-EFMMS, which made nNOS selectively approach the material compared with the other impurities and promoted covalent binding of nNOS to the epoxy groups, consequently, realizing simultaneous purification and covalent immobilization of His-nNOS.

To further study the functional groups on surface and the composition of EFMMS, XPS spectral analysis was performed. XPS analysis showed that EFMMS consisted of four elements including C, O, Si and N (Fig. 3 ). According to XPS pattern of four elements, it demonstrated that C atom exhibited three different states like C-C, C=C and C-O, O atom existed as C=O, H-O, and Si-O, all of which are functional groups from EFMMS. Additionally, two Si atom states including Si-C and Si-O could be analyzed from XPS image of Si 2p. It was worth noting that the peaks at 399.0 eV and 401.9 eV in XPS image of N1s showed the existence of N-H and CO-NH, which in turn testified the effective combination of APTES-CDs.

As illustrated in Fig. 3f, EDS spectral analysis also indicated the existence of C, N, O, Fe, Ni, Si, S and other elements in His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS, confirming the successful preparation of His-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS.

As a novel analytical technique, the ratio fluorescence probe could further improve the accuracy and sensitivity of the detection system compared with single-emission fluorescent probes [42]. To establish ratio fluorescence, red carbon dots were synthesized to pair with GFP-PSD95 [36]. Since rCDs contained abundant carboxyl groups, APTES-CDs were synthesized through EDC/NHS-catalyzed condensation between APTES amino groups and rCDs carboxyl groups. Furthermore, APTES-CDs bound with TEOS via APTES to be further encapsulated by the mesoporous silica layer to enhance the fluorescence stability of APTES-CDs (Fig. S4).

Under 475 nm excitation, GFP-PSD95 emitted at the optimal emission wavelength of 506 nm and red carbon dots emitted at 632 nm for the nano-biosensor PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS. Therefore, under the action of PSD95-nNOS uncoupling agent, GFP-PSD95 fell off from the material and was rapidly separated by magnetic, the residual PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was collected to determine fluorescence at 506 nm and 632 nm to obtain the ratio fluorescence intensity (RF intensity). The ratiometric detection system utilized PSD95 fluorescence at 506 nm as the detection signal, which changed upon uncoupler interaction, while red carbon dot emission at 632 nm served as a stable reference signal. This configuration enabled quantitative evaluation of uncoupling efficiency through ratio value changes.
The ratio fluorescence analysis for PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was investigated using the known PSD95-nNOS uncoupling agent ZL006 (Fig. 4 a).

The results of uncoupling experiment showed that the ratio fluorescence F506/F632 of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS decreased with the increase of ZL006 concentration. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, when the concentration of ZL006 was from 0.25 to 4 mM, the values of F506/F632 and the concentration of ZL006 presented a good linear relationship with the fitting linear equation y = -0.1335x + 1.3781 (R2 = 0.9907). The uncoupling efficiency of ZL006 at different concentrations was calculated according to formula (3). In the concentration range from 0.25 to 4 mM, the uncoupling efficiency rose with the increase of ZL006 concentration, presenting a good linear relationship with the fitted linear equation y = 23.2676x + 0.5278 (R2 = 0.9907). In summary, PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS could be established as an efficient and highly reliable in vitro screening model to explore PSD95-nNOS uncoupling agent.

The ratio fluorescence stability of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of free PSD95-nNOS lost up to 20 %, while the ratio fluorescence intensity of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS in PBS buffer could be stabilized around 100 % after standing at 4°C for 24 h (Fig. 5 a). The ratio fluorescence of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was more stable than the single fluorescence intensity of free PSD95-nNOS. Therefore, the evaluation of uncoupling PSD95 and nNOS through the ratio fluorescence of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS could enhance the stability and accuracy of fluorescence detection.

In addition, considering that the solubility of candidates had an impact on fluorescence detection, PBS buffer containing 0.1 % DMSO was selected as the solvent to ensure that all the candidates could be solved, accordingly, the ratio fluorescence stability of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS in PBS buffer containing 0.1 % DMSO was evaluated. Fig. 5a showed that 0.1 % DMSO didn’t influence the ratio fluorescence stability of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS.

To investigate pH stability of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS, PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was separately dispersed in 300 µL PBS buffers with different pH including 4, 5, 6, 7.4, 8, 9 and 10. At the same time, in order to simulate the experimental conditions of uncoupling, the suspension was uniformly incubated at 4°C and 125 rpm for 5 h, and the supernatant was removed by magnetic separation. As described in Fig. 5b, the ratio fluorescence intensity of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS could always be maintained above 80 % even under the condition of pH 4 or 10, which reflected the stability of protein immobilization and ratio fluorescence. Among all pH conditions, the ratio fluorescence intensity was the strongest at pH 8, which indicated that PBS buffer at pH 8 had the least influence on the fluorescence of APTES-CDs and GFP-PSD95, and wouldn’t destroy the conformation of proteins. Therefore, PBS buffer at pH 8 was selected as the best solvent condition.

The stability of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS in the solutions of metal ions like Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+ was investigated. At the same time, the impurities such as cellulose, hyaluronic acid, glucose and heteroproteins may exist in the solution of candidates, the anti-interference ability of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS against these compounds was evaluated. Fig. 5c indicated that Zn2+ and Co2+ slightly influenced the ratio fluorescence intensity of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS, and each of their ratio fluorescence intensity decreased to 88.02 % and 89.28 % compared with the blank group. However, the ratio fluorescence intensity could still be maintained above 90 % under the interference of the other metal ions. Additionally, PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS had strong anti-interference to impurities and heteroproteins, and always kept the ratio fluorescence intensity more than 95 % (Fig. 5d). The above results illustrated that PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS had strong anti-interference ability.

To establish an effective screening system, it was crucial that nNOS/Ni-EFMMS could be recovered and recycled. Therefore, GFP-PSD95 was uncoupled by ZL006 from PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS and re-coupled with nNOS/Ni-EFMMS to form PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS again. This process could monitor ratio fluorescence intensity to assess nNOS/Ni-EFMMS reusability. The ratio fluorescence intensity of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS remained above 90 % after seven cycles, indicating that nNOS/Ni-EFMMS had good stability and reusability compared with our previous study (Fig. 5e) [17].

According to the above studies, the stability and reliability of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS as a biosensor for discovering potential uncouplers in vitro had been proved. Therefore, it was applied to screen potential uncoupling compounds from the active ingredients of traditional Chinese medicine. Based on traditional Chinese medicine knowledge and previous findings on natural compounds for ischemic stroke treatment [1,15,16], as well as compounds identified through molecular imprinting technology that exhibit structural similarity to the positive control drug ZL006 [43]. Additionally, we had developed multifunctional metal–organic framework nanoparticles (PSD95-nNOS/Fe-MOF) for screening potential uncoupling agents in our previous protocol. This work revealed that several natural bioactive components exhibited potent PSD95-nNOS uncoupling activity. According to these findings, fourteen candidates involving the potential to treat ischemic stroke as PSD95-nNOS uncouplers were chosen and their chemical structures are shown in Fig. S5. PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was separately dispersed in the solutions of candidates to perform the uncoupling reaction. All the concentrations of compounds were set at 2.0 mM with ZL006 as positive control. Fig. 5f demonstrated that the uncoupling efficiencies of gnetol, baicalein and emodin-8-O-β-D-glucoside were similar, which were significantly better than that of ZL006 at the same concentration. In addition, the uncoupling efficiencies of baicalin, sanguinarine and emodin-1-O-β-D-glucoside were also better than ZL006, while the uncoupling efficiency of resveratrol and emodin-3′-O-glucoside was slightly weaker than ZL006.

Based on PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS uncoupling screening model, several compounds with good uncoupling efficiency were successfully screened from natural active ingredients, especially gnetol, baicalein, and emodin-8-O-β-D-glucoside. To further verify their uncoupling efficiency, molecular docking analysis was performed. The coupling of PSD95 and nNOS was achieved by inserting the β-finger structure of nNOS (Leu107-Phe111) into the β fragment of PDZ2 of PSD95 (Leu170-Ala175). In addition, a salt bridge was formed between Arg121 of nNOS and Asp62 of nNOS, which played an important role in the formation and stability of β-finger structure [14,44]. Based on the above mechanism, the docking analysis of nNOS (PBD: 1QAV) coupling with PSD95 (PBD: 3GSL) was carried out. Fig. 6 a showed that the three-dimensional structure diagrams of nNOS and PSD95. The three-dimensional structure docking diagram of nNOS coupling with PSD95, among which the green part was the active sites of nNOS, and the yellow part was the active sites of PSD95 (Fig. 6b). To verify the excellent uncoupling efficiency of gnetol, baicalein and emodin-8-O-β-D-glucoside, PSD95 active pocket was used as the active sites for docking uncoupling agents in Fig. 6 c-e. Figures on the left denoted 3D view of PSD95 active pocket separately docking with gnetol, baicalein and emodin-8-O-β-D-glucoside, and the middle diagrams exhibited the interaction forces of three compounds with PSD95 active pocket. Based on the figures, gnetol, baicalein and emodin-8-O-β-D-glucoside could dock into PSD95 active pocket via two, one and four hydrogen bonds interaction with PSD95 amino acid residues, respectively, which indicated that emodin-8-O-β-D-glucoside had the strongest binding ability to PSD95. As described in Fig. 6b, nNOS coupling with PSD95 showed docking score −1290.71, demonstrating that two proteins were stably coupled. However, when nNOS docked with PSD95 which had separately interacted with gnetol, baicalein and emodin-8-O-β-D-glucoside, the docking scores were 904.082, 993.502 and 938.341, respectively (Fig. 6 c-e). The docking results showed that the coupling of two proteins became difficult. It is proved that nNOS couldn’t couple with PSD95 after gnetol, baicalein and emodin-8-O-β-D-glucoside occupy the active pocket of PSD95, thus implying that three compounds possess potential PSD95-nNOS uncoupling effect.

In summary, PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS was fabricated via one-stone-four-birds strategy though combining magnetic mesoporous silica, simultaneous purification and covalent immobilization of His-nNOS to couple with GFP-PSD95, and the construction of ratio fluorescence via constant rCDs and varying PSD95. Characterization and stability analysis confirmed the effective immobilization of nNOS on Ni-EFMMS and its stable coupling with PSD95, demonstrating excellent stability, anti-interference capability, and reusability. The stable ratio fluorescence detection system reliably evaluated PSD95-nNOS uncoupling efficiency, establishing PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS as a robust nano-biosensor. Therefore, PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS could be used as a rapid and high-throughput screening model to explore PSD95-nNOS uncouplers. Gnetol, baicalein, and emodin-8-O-β-D-glucoside with strong uncoupling efficiency have been screened out from fourteen natural active ingredients. The uncoupling efficiency of three ingredients was also verified by molecular docking, which further proved the reliability of PSD95-nNOS/Ni-EFMMS as the screening model. This study provides a new strategy for discovering efficient PSD95-nNOS uncouplers from natural active ingredients.
